quote: So like I said, a lot of it is because, you know, on those days when it is producing, a lot of that gets used up in real time. So the, as soon as it's producing, the house is just using it right away. So it can't get down to those levels if it's just being used up. Does that make sense?
stewart_read: The rep's explanation is plausible but may be incomplete. Dave is reading a percentage from his monitoring app — it's unclear whether that percentage represents grid dependency, battery state of charge, or something else entirely. The rep never asked what app or display Dave is reading, which means the explanation may be answering the wrong question. This is a knowledge gap that compounds across four re-asks: the rep is confidently explaining a mechanism that may not match what Dave is actually observing.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep recognized the question was unanswerable and was buying time to find a redirect. Rejected — the rep recycled the same explanation twice before admitting uncertainty at 03:47, which is inconsistent with a deliberate deferral strategy.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, ask the rep: 'What percentage was Dave actually reading — and what does that number represent on his monitoring system?' If the rep doesn't know, that's the gap. The coaching move is to ask a clarifying question earlier ('What app are you looking at, and what does that number represent?') before attempting an explanation. Admitting uncertainty sooner and routing to the closer is cleaner than recycling an explanation that isn't landing.

quote: am I banking? What is going to the electric company?
stewart_read: This is the earliest and clearest setup for the ComEd buyback frame. Dave is explicitly asking about net metering — whether his excess production is being credited back. This was the moment to introduce the 70% buyback reality and pivot to battery storage. Instead, the rep answered the mechanics question ('excess gets sent back to the grid') without surfacing the buyback rate gap. The battery upsell didn't arrive until 06:01 — nearly two minutes later — after Dave had already moved on to a new sub-question.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep was building toward the battery pitch and wanted to answer the mechanics question first to establish credibility before introducing the product. Possible, but the gap between 04:32 and 06:01 includes another full round of production confusion (05:27–05:52) that the earlier pivot would have short-circuited.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the 04:32 timestamp and ask: 'What was Dave actually asking here?' The answer is net metering. The ComEd buyback frame the rep eventually used at 07:06 was the right tool — the coaching gap is that it was available at 04:32 and wasn't deployed until nearly two minutes later. Earlier deployment likely shortens the confusion loop and gets to the appointment faster.

quote: No. I am never really looked into that. Okay. Would it be worth I I mean, it's very expensive. I know the batteries ain't cheap. So would that be worth it?
stewart_read: Dave raises a cost objection on batteries immediately after the rep introduces the product. The rep's response at 06:42 is an explanation of battery benefits — what they store, when they help, how they work with ComEd's buyback rate. This is a features-and-benefits answer to a cost objection. The inversion available here: Dave is already paying the cost of NOT having a battery every month through the ComEd buyback gap the rep just described. The rep had the frame (70% buyback) but used it as a feature explanation rather than a cost flip. Per coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion: 'What's expensive is NOT switching.' The rep's answer was not wrong — it was just the safe move when the high-leverage move was available.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep chose explanation over inversion deliberately because Dave is an existing customer and a hard flip might feel adversarial. Possible — but the rep's explanation at 06:42 is generic ('based on your situation') rather than personalized to Dave's specific ComEd gap, which suggests the safe move was habitual rather than strategic.
coaching_implication: This is the floor's universal coaching gap per the schema: bill captured, inversion available, safe move taken instead. In the 1-on-1, walk the rep through the flip structure: Dave said batteries are expensive — the rep already knew Dave was losing money on the ComEd buyback rate. The coaching question: 'What's the cost of NOT having the battery, in Dave's specific situation?' The rep doesn't need a number to make this move — the frame is 'you're already paying for it, just to ComEd instead of to a battery.' Flag: this leverage point would land harder if the rep knew Dave's actual monthly bill and the ComEd buyback differential — coaching gap on financials.

quote: I know I got a tree in front, but since then, I have trimmed the tree, so it'll get more sunlight.
stewart_read: Dave has proactively removed a shading obstacle to improve his system's production. This is a strong engagement signal — he's invested enough in the system to take physical action to optimize it. The rep walked past this entirely, continuing the production-explanation loop. This signal also has cross-sell relevance: a customer who trimmed a tree to improve solar production is a customer who cares about maximizing output — exactly the profile for a battery storage conversation. The rep eventually got to the battery pitch, but without anchoring it to Dave's demonstrated investment in the system.
coaching_implication: Show the rep this line and ask: 'What does it tell you about Dave that he trimmed a tree to get more sun on his panels?' The answer is: he's an engaged, invested customer who wants the system to perform. That's the anchor for the battery pitch — not 'ComEd doesn't give you 100% back' (generic) but 'you already took steps to produce more — a battery means you actually keep what you produce instead of giving it to ComEd at a discount.' The rep had the raw material and didn't use it.

quote: so looks like he he gets back Monday afternoon. So he'll be in Tuesday. Is there a good time Tuesday that would work for you for him to give you a call or would make it a good day be better? If that's the fifth. If that's the fifth
stewart_read: The rep books the callback but the button-up is structurally incomplete. Missing elements per the schema: (1) no one-hour block stated, (2) no 'no driving / at computer' anchor (less critical for a phone callback but the principle of setting expectations holds), (3) no 'is that fair?' verbal handshake, (4) no text follow-up promise. The closer's name is also ambiguous — the rep says 'Kate' at 07:36 and 'Tay' at 08:09 and 08:51. The transcript does not resolve this. The handoff brief flags this as a data-quality issue.
coaching_implication: Walk the rep through the button_up checklist against this call. The missing text follow-up is the most consequential gap — this callback is booked over a weekend with a customer who said 'just to see.' Without a text confirmation, there is no artifact keeping the appointment alive between now and Tuesday at 11AM. Also flag the name ambiguity: the rep should know the closer's name and confirm it clearly on the call.

quote: so looks like he he gets back Monday afternoon. So he'll be in Tuesday.
stewart_read: No 'is that fair?' verbal handshake at close. Per the schema, this is a required button_up element predictive of show rate. Dave's agreement ('Alright. Yeah. I'd like to talk to him') is voluntary but the handshake is the verbal contract that converts passive agreement into active commitment. Its absence on a fragile 'just to see' booking is a meaningful gap.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep read Dave as already committed and skipped the handshake to avoid over-formalizing a warm close. Possible — but the schema is explicit that this element is required precisely because it's predictive of show rate, not because it's needed when the customer seems warm.
coaching_implication: Pair this with the text follow-up gap in the 1-on-1. The coaching point: two of the three artifacts that keep a fragile callback alive (verbal handshake + text confirmation) are missing. The rep got the booking — the question is whether it holds through the weekend.

quote: K. Sounds good, mister Bennett. Appreciate your time. And, yeah, any other questions about the percentage is too, feel free to ask Tate about that. He'll have he might help you out there as well. So Okay. That'll be good. Thank you, sir. Thank you. Have a great weekend. Yeah. You too. Bye.
stewart_read: The call closes without a text follow-up promise. Per the schema, the text after the call is a required button_up element — and for this specific booking (a weekend callback with a cautious customer), the text confirmation is the primary artifact keeping the appointment alive until Tuesday. The rep also routes Dave's unresolved production question to the closer ('feel free to ask Tate about that') — which is appropriate scope discipline — but the name '[Tate / Kate — name unclear from transcript]' is ambiguous and should be flagged. If Dave receives a call Tuesday from someone whose name doesn't match what he was told, it creates a legitimacy friction point.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1: 'What's the one thing that keeps this appointment alive between now and Tuesday?' The answer is the text. Walk the rep through the text_after_call template and ask them to draft what they would have sent Dave — it should reference the tree trimming, the ComEd buyback gap, and the closer's name (once confirmed). Also resolve the name ambiguity before the closer calls Dave.
